title: Hormidium humile
taxonomy: Hormidium humile is classified as a type of lichenized fungus, belonging to the family Parmeliaceae. It is a species of crustose lichen, which grows as a crust or patch on a rock or other surface. The classification of Hormidium humile also includes its placement within the genus Hormidium and the kingdom Fungi. This classification helps scientists understand its evolutionary history and relationships with other organisms. Further research may be necessary to confirm and refine the classification of Hormidium humile.
uses: Hormidium humile is known for its potential medicinal uses, including its anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. Research suggests that extracts from the plant may have wound-healing properties and be effective in treating skin infections. Additionally, studies have shown that Hormidium humile may have antioxidant and anti-cancer properties, making it a subject of interest in the field of cancer research. However, more research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses of this plant. Further studies are necessary to confirm its effectiveness and potential side effects.

intro: Hormidium humile, also known as Indian Madder or Rubia cordifolia, is a perennial plant belonging to the Rubiaceae family, traditionally used in Ayurvedic and Unani medicine. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial properties, which may help in reducing fever, treating skin conditions, and supporting digestive health. The bioactive constituents of Hormidium humile include anthraquinones, alkaloids, and flavonoids, such as rubiadin, purpurogallin, and rubiadin-1-methyl ether, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. In traditional medicine, Hormidium humile is often used to prepare herbal preparations, including decoctions, infusions, and powders, which are used to treat a range of health conditions, including fever, rheumatism, and skin disorders.
